content_html:
<pre class="source-text">INF10025Data Management and Analytics
Week 02 – Part 3
Forms, Indexes

Adding a new record to the Movie Table
Last lecture we created a Movie table.  To add a new movie we:
Open the Movie Datasheet view
Click on the * at the bottom left of the view
Type in the details
The Datasheet view:
Not end-user friendly. You would not expect end-users/customer to use a datasheet.
If this was an employee table, it would contain personal details. The end-user could see data about other employees
Easy to delete data in one row / multiple rows very easily (by mistake)

The Movie Table
The Movie table has a field called RatingCode.
What is a "PG" rated movie?
RatingName: "PARENTAL GUIDANCE"
Description: "Parental guidance recommended for persons under 15 years."
What is a "MA" rated movie?
RatingName: "MATURE ACCOMPANIED (15+)"
Description: "Persons under 15 years must be accompanied by a mature adult."

Movies and Rating Codes
When more than one table exists in a Database, we should open the Relationships Manager
We add the tables to themanager window

Movies and Rating Codes
There is a connection between Movie and Rating.Drag the field RATINGCODE in the movie table and drop it over RATINGCODE in the Rating table

Movies and Rating Codes

The movie.ratingcode field is referred to asa Foreign Key.
Each Foreign Key value in the Movie
table references a Primary Key value
in the Rating Table

Query based on a two table relationship
Now design a new Query using both the Movie and Rating tables.
As you add the tables to the query, the relationship is shown.
This query has data from both tables. The query matches every FK value in the Movie table with a PK value in the Rating table.

Referential Integrity
The option to Enforce Referential Integrity
is a constraint that the RDBMS performs to
only allow values in the movie.ratingcode
field that match an existing value in the
rating table
Entering a movie.ratingcode value of 'X'
would cause a referential integrity error.

Be aware of unrelated tables
Imagine that you did not create the relationship between the Movie and Rating tables.
They are simply two unrelated tables.
When you create a query, the RDBMS does not know what these tables have in common.
There is no connection between the rating codes in the two tables (at least that's what the RDBMS thinks).

Be aware of Cartesian Products
If you run a query that uses data from both unrelated tables, then a 'cartesian product' is created.    Every row in movie table matches every row in the ratings table.
318 movies * 5 ratings = 1590 rows!    Utterly useless.     Can be very slow to create. AVOID

Movie Table /  ColourType relationship
The Movie Table has a field called Colour_Code
Imagine that we create a table named ColourType.
This must be added to the Relationship Manager.
We have two Many to One relationships.

ACTOR, MOVIE relations
Bradley Cooper acted in the movie titled "Silver Linings Playbook"
I.e. actorNo 51329 acted in movieNo 82693

I.e. actorNo 72129 acted in movieNo 82693
Jennifer Lawrence acted in the movie "The Hunger Games"

We can store this information using these codes
Each row shown here represents one actor cast into one movie

Casting table
We will create a table called Casting.
Each record in this table represents One Actor in One Movie
The CastId is simply a auto generated number to give each row a unique identity (a primary key value).

Relationship Diagram
This is an updated Relationship Diagram. It looks complicated.
However, it is simply a collection of multiple Many to One relationships.
Each relationship has a Foreign Key that references a Primary Key

Totals
Often, when users want to retrieve data from database tables, they want to display some totals
How many employees do we have?
How many students are enrolled in INF10002?
How many staff of each gender do we have?
In the database world, this is often referred to as Aggregation
Typical total (or aggregation) functions are Count, Sum, Min, Max, Average

Count Values
Requirement: Count how many movies belong to each rating code
The answer should be in this format
G	   24
PG	   45
M	 121
MA   83
R	     5
This query must display the Rating Code in column 1
This query must display the Count value in column 2

Totals
In Query Design Ribbon, click on the Totals icon
This adds a Total row to the Query Design Grid

Totals
Add the field RatingCode to the Design Grid.
The Total operation says Group By.
This means that all of the RatingCodes in the
records will be Grouped.
G is a group
PG is a group
MA is a group…

Totals
If we ran this query as it is, then we simply have a list of RatingCodes that appear in the Movie table.
Not really useful.

Totals
Add the field RatingCode for a second time
to the Design Grid.
This time change the type of Total
(or aggregation) you want to perform
In this example, we will selectCount from the pull down menu
Running the queryshows the count valuefor each ratingcode.

Changing the column heading
The column heading CountOfRATINGCODE is not very professional
Add a new column heading by placingthe new heading followed by a semicolon : character prefix in front of the field name
E.g.     Total: RATINGCODE
Note: This technique applies to any column heading in any query that you create

Another example of Group By
This is a similar example.
In this case, the query groups records by relyear.
For each relyear value, the total number of records is counted.

Two columns with Group By
Requirement: Count the number of movies in each rating code for each release year.
The 1st column groups data by Relyear
The 2nd column groups data by RatingCode
within a Relyear
The 3rd column shows the count of movies foreach group combination.
2016 has 11 movies
Of those:
7 have an M rating
2 have a PG rating
1 has a G rating
1 has a MA rating

Two columns with Group By
This example demonstrates the AVG aggregation
function.
The average TMDB score is calculated for each year.

Two columns with Group By
The round the AVG calculation, we can use the Round() function
Round() requires
the value that you want to round
the number of decimal places required
Write the expression in the Field cell:

Two columns with Group By
Access automatically adds the column name prefix Expr1.
You can replace this prefix with any heading that you want

Two columns with Group By
With any of the aggregate functions you can include a criteria that is applied to the totals generated by the aggregate functions.
In this case, the query will only show totals wherethe average TMDB score is greater than or equal to 7.4

Access Forms
Access allows developers to quickly and easily create input forms
This form only shows one
record at a time.
Easy for the end-user to focus on one item
Difficult to delete / make changes to multiple records(by mistake).

Creating a simple form
Select Form Wizard
Specify which columns are to appear on the form.
(Some columns that are       calculated or populated at a       later date may not have to       appear)

Creating a simple form
Choose the type of form required	Columnar allows you to place and resize        all fields
Finally, give the form a name e.g. Movie Form

The Movie Database
In a more professional system, you could add a menu or buttons that make it easier to perform CRUD (create, read, update, delete) operations. You could add colour, text, images, hints etc.
Note:
Forms with buttons, menu, hints, validations, user defined error messages, sub forms, etc. are not 'simple to create'. There are many books, tutorials & discussion boards that cover these topics.

Indexes
You may have noticed that wheneverwe created a Primary Key column, the field property panel populated
the property Indexed.
What is an index?
How can it assist us?

Indexes
Imagine that this is a list of employee ids.
Do these employeesexist?
212
61
It is hard & slow workto sequentially viewevery value.

Indexes
Do these employeesexist?
91
502
The search is much easier when the values are stored in
numeric sequence.

Indexes
If the index did not exist, Access would have to view each record in the employee table sequentially to find a matching employee id.
With 100,000 records, that could be a long time. (Well a second or two).
If there were 10 million rows, the delay would increase.
The more rows we have…. the slower the sequential search.

Indexes
Suppose we create an index on the Employee Id of the employee tableThe table currently has 100,000 records.
Access creates an index which stores
The index value (the employee id)
The location of the record within the database
When Access searches for an employee id
It quickly finds the employee id in the index (if it exists)
Gets the location of the record
Retrieves the record

Primary Key Index
Whenever you create a Primary key, Access automatically creates a Primary Key index.
If you enter a Primary Key value that has already been used by another record, Access displays a Duplicate Primary Key error message.
One of the reasons Access can determine if there is a duplicate primary key so quickly is that the field is indexed. Finding a matching key value id simple and quick.

Sequential Record Access
Read each record one by one from the beginning of the table until therequired record is found.
The DBMS may have to evaluate 50,000 separate records.
Assume that each record is very large.
Assume each evaluation requires one disk I/O. *
Disk I/O is the slowest computer activity.
On average, a search for an employee will require 25,000 disk I/Os.
* - In reality, a DBMS will read records as blocks of data (e.g. 2K).     Therefore many records can be read into memory     with a single disk I/O

Inserting data into a simple Index
When a table has a Primary Key, an Index based on that Primary Key is automatically created.
As each row is added, the DBMS automatically inserts the search key and the row location information into the index file.
Insert 1st row. Employee 109
A row is inserted into the Employee table
The search key and row location is added to the PK index

Obviously, the Index is much smaller than the table data
Searching the index is quicker than sequentially searching the table
The Index is in key sequence. This makes searching quick.

Locating the row for employee 289 is quicker using the index.
A RDBMS may use a search method on the index such as a Binary Search
A Binary Search continues to split the index in half until a match is/is not found
Start at the middleth position
The value being searched for is either
The current key value  OR
A key value higher in the list   OR
A key value lower in the list
At least half of the key values are eliminated.The eliminated values do not need to be searched

Imagine that you want to publish all 100,000 employees in Employee ID sequence
The database will jump from location to location to retrieve the data in the correct sequence.
Employee 4 is at location 97,491
Employee 5 is at location 18
Employee 7 is at location 43,292 …
The RDBMS is doing a large amount of work
If the table was not indexed:
How would the RDBMS find the employee with the lowest ID?
How would the RDBMS find the next lowest ID? …
It would be very, very slow.
(In reality, RDBMS are very quick at processing records. If a table only has a few thousand rows, the speed of using an index or using a sequential search will almost be identical.)

Advantages of using an Index
Using an index is a quick method to locate rows.
Large portions of the index (or the entire index) may be read into the computer's memory at once before searching for rows in a table.
Fewer disk I/Os (input/outputs – the slowest function of a computer).
This reduces the number of disk I/Os required to read table rows
This speeds up retrieval time
In the above example, finding employee 289 took 2 disk I/Os.
One to read the index into RAM
One to read the 11th row in the table.

Additional information
A binary search is only one type of search method available to a DBMS
In reality, far more complex indexing options exist
Most RDBMS may have multiple options / methods of indexing tables
The option selected often depends on an understanding of the data values that will be stored.
It is not possible to display the contents of an index
Indexes may be stored in a non-human readable format and/or  a compressed format to reduce their size and improve search speeds

Additional information
The index does not contain a record number.
The record number we see in the datasheet doesn't really exist!
Fred Blogs data may be stored in Record 3. If Record 1 & 2 are deleted, then Fred becomes Record 1.
So the Record Number is a poor way of identifying a record
(That's one reason we use Primary Keys).
Instead, the RDBMS uses a physical address on disk to specify the location of the record.  This is the value of RowLoc in the previous slides
In Oracle RDBMS the address is something like AAAYUzAAEAABIjLAAJ
Access makes it almost impossible for you to view the physical address.
Students studying Database Systems INF20010/60014 learn moreabout this topic in the Data Warehousing module.

Index Creation
It is relatively easy to create an Index.
This is automatic for Primary Keys.
Some indexes can have duplicate value.
Consider a surname field:
Surname is not a good Primary Key  (too many people share a surname)
However it makes sense to create an index on surname
A surname is often searched for  or  reports are required in surname sequence
Using a binary search (or similar) will still speed up finding the 1st row with a match surname value

SQL: Multiple indexes (indices)
Suppose we want to execute this query
How many rows have to be sequentially read if the table has 100,000 rows(with no index on surname) to find a match?
On average 50,000 reads
How many reads are required if there is an index based on Surname? Far fewer!
One read to load index into RAM. Perform binary search.
One read to retrieve the first matching row
Subsequent reads for any duplicate surnames

Indexes speed up report generation
There are many IT database stories involving reports that
Use many tables with Foreign Key lookups
Involve millions or billions of database records
Take hours to produce
A daily report that takes more than 10 hours to run may be next to useless.
Such reports do exist!
Creating indexes on fields central to the report have often reduced report generation time from many hours to a few minutes.

The cost of creating Indexes
Why not index every field?:
Disk Space  - a minor consideration. Disk space is cheap.
Update-Time - a major consideration.
When records in a table are inserted, changed, deleted:
Every index must be updated
so that the index remains in key sequence
Each change takes time to complete
Too many indexes can make unacceptable delays
Imagine each Unit that you enrol in, causes a few seconds delay
As all of the indexes are updated
Imagine each time Australia Census data is recorded there is a delay as all indexes are updated
Users will not be happy

Deletion / Recreation of Indexes?
Database management in an organisation often involves compromises
An organisation may run a once-a-week report.
The report requires many indexes to run acceptably
It may make sense to
Create the indexes prior to running the report
it make take a few minutes for each index to be created
Run the report
Drop the indexes
Often such tasks can be scheduled to automatically take place

It's an index of indexes.
Task: Find employee 145

DBMS Optimisers
DBMS systems have optimisers.
Optimisers are designed to decide the quickest and most efficient way of accessing data.
Multiple indexes may exist, but it is the DBMS optimiser that decides if it is of benefit to use the indexes.
Image we have a table with a small number of rows.We have indexes based on the Employee Id and Surname.
Imagine that it only takes one or two reads to load the entire table into memory,
It may be quicker to read/load all rows into memorycompared to reading / loading the Indexes into memory and then retrieving the appropriate row(s)
Optimisers attempt to reduce the time taken to process the query.</pre>
